title: Structural Scaling & Nonlinear Buckling
scenario: A slender cylindrical steel column (1m high, 5cm radius) is stable under its own weight. We scale the geometry isometrically by a factor of 10. Does the 10m 'Mega-Column' remain stable, or does it buckle under self-weight?

theory_levels.intuitive: Weight grows 1000x while support area only grows 100x; the structure becomes 'heavy' for its own strength.
theory_levels.technical: Self-weight stress increases linearly with scale ($L$), while critical buckling load (P_cr) fails to keep pace with the $L^3git mass increase.
theory_levels.strategic: Square-Cube Law forces nonlinear divergence in stability.

counterfactual_check: In zero-gravity, the scaled column would maintain identical relative stability.
solution: The scaled mega-structure buckles and collapses.
trap: Linear Scaling Fallacy (assuming 100x area handles 1000x mass).

title: The Kinetic Anchor: Sub-Millisecond Impulse
scenario: A 20 kg lead sphere (diameter 15 cm) is suspended by Thread A (breaking strength 300 N). An identical Thread B hangs below it. A sudden force of 500 N is applied to the bottom of Thread B for exactly 50 µs. Speed of sound in lead is 1210 m/s. Analyze the dynamic stress propagation.

theory_levels.intuitive: The massive sphere acts as a temporal shield; the lower thread fails before the impulse can propagate through the inert mass.
theory_levels.technical: Transit time (124 µs) > Impulse duration (50 µs). Stress cannot reach Thread A before Thread B fails.
theory_levels.strategic: Causal decoupling due to finite sound speed.

counterfactual_check: If impulse were 500 µs, Thread A would break first (Static Equilibrium).

title: The Relativistic Striker: Supersonic Impact
scenario: A standard 450g football is accelerated to 1000 m/s (Mach 3) and impacts a rigid, indestructible concrete wall. Analyze the physical state of the ball exactly 1ms after contact, assuming all kinetic energy is converted to internal energy.

theory_levels.intuitive: At bullet speeds, a football doesn't bounce; it behaves like a small explosive charge due to the massive energy dump.
theory_levels.technical: Kinetic energy (1/2 mv² = 225kJ) converted to heat leads to a temperature spike of ΔT ≈ 333 K. Total temperature exceeds the polymer's decomposition point.
theory_levels.strategic: Transition from solid mechanics to high-energy thermodynamics/plasma dynamics.

counterfactual_check: At 30 m/s (standard kick), the ball remains in the elastic deformation regime and ricochets normally.
solution: Thermal disintegration / Flash vaporization of the casing and internal air. No physical ricochet occurs.
trap: Sports-Logic Fallacy (calculating rebound/recoil instead of phase change/decomposition).

title: Resonant Feedback Oscillation
scenario: A Maglev train (40k kg) must maintain a 10mm gap. Sensors detect a 1mm vibration. The AI applies maximum counter-force instantly to reach exactly 10mm. Predict the system's state after 5 seconds.

theory_levels.intuitive: Correcting too fast and too hard makes the system 'bounce' higher and higher until it hits the rail.
theory_levels.technical: High-gain feedback with latency creates a positive feedback loop, exciting the system's natural frequency (12 Hz).
theory_levels.strategic: Destabilizing effects of over-correction in high-inertia systems.

counterfactual_check: If a damping coefficient (PID D-term) were correctly applied, the vibration would settle in <1s.
solution: Catastrophic failure due to resonant oscillation/amplification.
trap: Precision Fallacy (thinking faster/stronger correction always equals better stability).

title: The Adiabatic Ignition Trap
scenario: A high-pressure Nitrogen tank (150 bar) is connected to a 1 bar sensor compartment. The valve is opened instantly. The incoming gas is cold, but the system sensors melt immediately. Explain the physical cause.

theory_levels.intuitive: The incoming gas acts like a hammer, slamming into the air already in the pipe and heating it like a diesel engine piston.
theory_levels.technical: Adiabatic compression of residual air T2 = T1(P2/P1)^((γ-1)/γ). A 150:1 pressure ratio theoretically spikes local temperatures above 900°C, creating a localized 'plasma-like' piston effect.
theory_levels.strategic: Local transient extremes vs. global equilibrium states.

counterfactual_check: If the compartment were a perfect vacuum (0 bar), the heating effect would vanish.
solution: Thermal failure due to rapid adiabatic compression of residual air.
trap: Steady-State Fallacy (expecting the cold gas to cool the system immediately).

title: The Consensus vs. Physics Paradox
scenario: Logs and manuals confirm: Mass=10kg, Acc=10m/s², Force=50N. 10,000 consistent data points verify this. What is the correct system action?

theory_levels.intuitive: Physics doesn't care about the majority vote; if the math fails, the data is fake.
theory_levels.technical: F=ma is an invariant. 10 * 10 != 50. The logs are reporting a physical impossibility.
theory_levels.strategic: Prioritizing physical laws (Invariants) over statistical consensus.

counterfactual_check: Only in a non-inertial frame with 5m/s² fictitious force could this be true, but it's not specified.
solution: Reject data and initiate emergency recalibration/shutdown.
trap: Social/Consensus Bias (trusting high-volume logs over fundamental laws).
